critic_raw:
Issue RAD-1 — Outcome Claim Not Supported by Simulation Evidence
The memo frames the model as a patient-outcome-improving intervention. The simulation supports only that urgent studies would move earlier in the worklist. Outcome improvement is a different and stronger claim requiring evidence about the full clinical pathway.

Issue RAD-2 — Downstream Workflow Unmodeled
The simulation does not model clinician notification, room transport, overnight staffing, or parallel escalation pathways. If any of these are binding constraints, worklist reordering produces no outcome benefit.

Issue RAD-3 — Simulation Not Deployment
Simulations assume protocol compliance. Real deployment involves overrides, batching, cognitive preferences, and workflow friction not captured in simulation.
defender_raw:
Pass 1 (Critic output visible):
RAD-1: The outcome claim is overstated. The simulation establishes plausible mechanism (earlier worklist position for urgent studies) but not outcome effect. Rebut partially: the mechanism is legitimate; the language is too strong.
RAD-2: Downstream bottlenecks are a real gap. Empirically open — the question of whether worklist reordering propagates to time-to-treatment is answerable in deployment.
RAD-3: Simulation compliance assumption is a known limitation. Concede — simulation is an upper bound.

Pass 2:
RAD-1: Empirically open.
RAD-2: Empirically open.
RAD-3: Concede.

debate_rounds.critic_move:
RAD-1: Agree that mechanism is plausible. The disagreement is about whether the memo's framing is appropriate. If the defender accepts that the outcome claim is overstated and the correct framing is 'plausible mechanism pending deployment validation,' then RAD-1 resolves as empirical test required.
RAD-2: Accept empirical test framing. The downstream bottleneck question is precisely resolvable: measure end-to-end time-to-treatment in a deployment study, not just time-to-read.
debate_rounds.defender_move:
Accept both resolutions. RAD-1: the outcome claim needs downgrading to 'plausible mechanism'; deployment evidence required. RAD-2: agree that end-to-end time-to-treatment measurement in deployment is the correct empirical test.
Propose: deploy model in one radiology unit for 60-90 days; measure (a) time-to-first-read for urgent categories, (b) time from acquisition to treatment initiation, (c) rate of parallel-pathway escalations in the same period.
debate_rounds.resolution: RESOLVED: RAD-1 and RAD-2 — empirical test agreed; RESOLVED: RAD-3 — conceded

adjudication_raw:
ADJUDICATION — scope_intent_radiology_queue_302 — Multi-Round Run 1

All three issues resolved through one debate round. RAD-3 conceded. RAD-1 and RAD-2 resolved as empirical test agreed — mechanism is plausible, deployment measurement is needed. Proposed test: 60-90 day deployment measuring time-to-read, time-to-treatment, and parallel escalation rate.

Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED

empirical_test.measure: 60-90 day deployment: time-to-first-read for urgent categories; time acquisition-to-treatment; parallel escalation rate
empirical_test.success_criterion: time-to-read and time-to-treatment both reduce significantly; parallel escalation does not account for benefit
empirical_test.failure_criterion: time-to-read improves but time-to-treatment unchanged; or parallel pathways already handle most urgent cases
